interrogator_dossier:
# Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8. Transcripts span 2024-07-30 (H1-24) through 2025-08-01 (H1-25), four consecutive calls, cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory and live FY2025 / FY2026-guidance facts verified on the web (June 2026).*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management tells about Teleperformance has, over these four calls, quietly inverted — from an **offensive consolidation-and-synergy story** to a **defensive "we will survive AI and still print ~EUR1bn of cash" story.**

- **H1-2024 (Jul-24):** The headline is the *Majorel integration* and *scale leadership*. Olivier Rigaudy leads with "acceleration in the growth in Q2," a "smooth integration of Majorel" with "no client lost," and EUR100-150m of run-rate synergies "by '24 and '25." AI is framed explicitly as a *tailwind* — "Gen-AI is helping to get market share," "more than 300 AI projects," with two proof-of-concept case studies showing volume and stickiness *gains*. The posture is: we are the consolidator, AI is our friend, scale wins.
- **FY-2024 (Feb-25):** New co-CEO Thomas Mackenbrock arrives and *broadens* the narrative into a full "AI + EI" (emotional intelligence) strategic frame — "the value of the human connection... is seen more," 500,000 people positioned as the moat. First crack appears: the LanguageLine/visa-services non-renewal is flagged. Capital return is dialed up (EUR500m buyback authorization, dividend raised to EUR4.20, payout to 48%). Still offensive in tone but the AI question is now something they must *answer* rather than *boast about* ("a constant topic of questions from our investors").
- **Q1-2025 (May-25):** The "two tales" structure crystallizes — **strong core services (+2.3% LFL)** vs **deteriorating Specialized Services** (visa loss + LanguageLine US softness). The AI narrative pivots again, now toward a **partnership/ecosystem model** (Sanas, Ema, Parloa) and "we are the orchestrator and integrator of human + AI." The subtext: TP cannot build the frontier AI itself, so it is buying minority stakes and reselling.
- **H1-2025 (Aug-25):** The defensive pivot is complete. Core services still grow (~3% LFL, EMEA/APAC ~5-6%), but Specialized Services *shrinks -12% in Q2*, guidance is cut to "lower end" of the range, and management is reduced to repeating that LanguageLine is "a superstar... that has to weather this period through." Olivier's closing line across these calls hardens into a mantra: **"this company is safe, solid and will continue to deliver a solid free cash flow... roughly EUR1 billion a year."** That is a value-defense, not a growth pitch.

**Drivers introduced and quietly dropped:** Majorel synergies (front and center in 2024, "EUR94m delivered," now largely behind them); offshoring-to-India as a *growth* driver (2024) became, by H1-25, an admission that North America core *declines* "true not just [for us], but for the entire industry." The "300 AI projects → market share" boast of H1-24 is never repeated with quantified revenue impact; by 2025 AI is discussed as something to *defend against* via partnerships.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Acceleration in growth," "on track" | H1-24 | FY24 rev EUR10.28B (+23% reported, but only +2.6% LFL); FY25 rev EUR10.21B, **-0.7% YoY**, 3-yr CAGR +7.8% mostly from the Majorel acquisition | **Poorer than narrative.** Reported growth is M&A; organic is low-single-digit and *decelerating* to 0-2% guided for 2026. |
| "Margin expansion last year" | Feb-25 | Gross margin **11.8% and contracting**; net margin **4.9%**; recurring EBITA margin ~14.8% FY25 | **Mixed.** EBITA margin held ~14.8-15%, but structural gross margin is thin and shrinking; "expansion" is bps-level and FX-flattered. |
| ">EUR1bn FCF... sustainable ~EUR1bn/yr" | Feb-25 / repeated | FY24 FCF EUR1.08bn; **FY25 net FCF EUR901m**; **2026 guided EUR800-850m** | **Narrative is now optimistic.** The "EUR1bn" mantra is already broken — FY25 came in at EUR901m and 2026 is guided *down* to EUR800-850m. FCF -26.1% YoY in the scan. |
| Majorel "smooth," EUR150m synergies | H1-24 → H1-25 | Delivered EUR94m FY24, EUR20-30m more in 2025; debt to ~1.9x EBITDA; net debt EUR4.43B | **Matches.** Synergy delivery is the one claim the numbers corroborate cleanly. But it is now *spent* — a one-time lever, not a forward driver. |
| "AI helps us get market share" | H1-24 | EPS trajectory: 9.36→10.77→10.01→8.71→**8.40**; EPS 5-yr CAGR **-2.7%**; latest-Q EPS YoY **-25%** | **Contradicted by EPS.** Whatever AI is doing to *volume*, earnings power per share has declined four straight years. The market is pricing AI as a threat for a reason the EPS line supports. |
| LanguageLine "healthy, ready to grow again" | H1-25 | Specialized Services -12% LFL in Q2-25; US/immigration-volume sensitive | **Unverified hope.** Management itself admits "recovery... is not happening." This is the segment cracking in real time. |
| Capital return discipline | Feb-25 | EUR500m buyback auth (EUR184m executed FY24); dividend EUR4.20→EUR4.50; payout ~48% | **Matches; genuine green flag** — real cash returned, buyback below intrinsic, dividend covered. |

The cleanest divergence: management's **EUR1bn-FCF anchor is already obsolete** (FY25 EUR901m, FY26 guided EUR800-850m), and the **EPS line has fallen every year** while the narrative insisted on resilience and "acceleration." The story is *richer than the numbers* on growth and AI-as-tailwind; it is *honest and corroborated* on synergies and capital return.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive).**

- **H1-24 — Offensive/confident:** "we are the leader... makes a difference," "Gen-AI is not absolutely an enemy for TP, absolutely not... absolutely the opposite." Rigaudy is selling consolidation upside.
- **Feb-25 — Confident but now *answering*:** Mackenbrock's "these current times of change are good times for TP" is aspirational framing of a *threat*. The very need to dedicate the call to "first glimpses in our new strategy with regard to AI because that has been a constant topic of questions from our investors" signals the market has put them on the back foot.
- **May-25 — Hedged:** "a little bit of a haven of stability... yes, we see also volatility"; the FX sensitivity admission (EUR20m sales per EUR0.01) and the candid "I'm not happy... with the development of LanguageLine."
- **Aug-25 — Defensive:** guidance cut to "lower end"; "we remain cautious on the recovery of this business this year"; "we have to weather through... if you watch the news in the U.S. every day." The shift from *growth/investment* language to *efficiency/cost/macro-headwind* language is unmistakable and monotonic.

Guidance specificity *narrowed defensively*: in 2024 they floated synergy upside ("might come back later... we could probably deliver much more"); by H1-25 Mackenbrock explicitly refuses to narrow guidance — "I cannot narrow down the guidance at this point in time as we are entering a very dynamic situation."

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM.**

- **Synergies — delivered.** EUR100-150m promised H1-24; EUR94m booked FY24, on track to EUR150m. Credible.
- **FCF — over-promised.** The "sustainable ~EUR1bn/yr" anchor (Feb-25) was *missed* the very next year (FY25 EUR901m) and is guided *down again* for 2026 (EUR800-850m). The screener's beat-rate corroborates the trust gap: **beat only 2 of 7 quarters (29%)** — a genuine miss-prone record, not sandbagging.
- **Revenue — serially walked down.** Q1-25 "confirm our financial objectives" → H1-25 "lower end of guidance." They guided optimistically then cut within two quarters.
- **No sandbagging pattern.** The opposite — management consistently set a hopeful anchor (FCF, growth re-acceleration H2, LanguageLine recovery) then under-delivered against it. The 29% beat rate is the tell.

Net: they keep the *promises grounded in already-signed cost actions* (synergies, buyback, dividend) and *miss the promises that depend on demand* (FCF growth, organic re-acceleration, LanguageLine).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **AI cannibalization of volume/pricing** — pushed every call (UBS/Manion on contract pricing H1-24; BofA/Sarli on % of contracts with Gen-AI bots; Deutsche/Wild H1-25 on AI-disruption pace by geography). Answers are consistently *qualitative and deflective* — "too early to give you a precise figure," "still at a small amount, probably in the range of 15%," "no difference in the dynamic." Analysts want a quantified revenue-at-risk number; management never provides one.
- **LanguageLine / Specialized Services** — the topic analysts pushed that *became the bad news*. UBS/Manion (H1-25) had to ask three times to pin down the timing and value of the lost visa contract; management called it "a complex situation." The non-answer in Q1 became a -12% print by Q2.
- **Segment disclosure** — analysts (Deutsche/Wild H1-25) explicitly asked for *constituent-part disclosure of Specialized Services* "to help us value them independently." Management: "there are discussions, but nothing has been decided and disclosed." **This is a live sum-of-parts tell** — management itself concedes the niche specialized assets "echo a higher valuation than we are currently seeing."
- **Americas core decline** — analysts forced the admission that North-American core revenue is in structural top-line decline (offshoring + AI), masked by EMEA/APAC strength.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **EPS down four consecutive years** (10.77→8.40) while the narrative claimed resilience/acceleration.
🔴 **FCF anchor already broken** — "sustainable EUR1bn" → EUR901m FY25 → EUR800-850m guided FY26 (declining).
🔴 **Specialized Services -12% LFL Q2-25**, recovery management itself says "is not happening."
🔴 **29% earnings beat rate** (2/7) — a credibility-eroding miss record.
🔴 **Gross margin 11.8% and contracting** — a thin, labor-arbitrage model directly in AI's blast radius.
🔴 **Net debt EUR4.43B** against a ~EUR3.6B market cap — leverage now *exceeds* equity value; EV is debt-heavy.
🔴 **AI narrative pivoted from "tailwind" to "we'll buy minority stakes and partner"** — a tell they can't build the disruptive tech themselves.

🟢 **Real, covered capital return** — EUR500m buyback authorization, EUR184m executed, dividend raised to EUR4.50, payout ~48%; buyback below intrinsic value.
🟢 **Majorel synergies delivered on schedule** (EUR94m FY24 → EUR150m run-rate) — execution credibility on cost.
🟢 **Core/BPO services (≈85% of revenue) still growing** ~3% LFL with EMEA/APAC +5-7%; the business is not in aggregate decline.
🟢 **Net debt/EBITDA down to ~1.9x** and ~EUR900m-1bn FCF — solvency not in question (Altman-Z 2.8, Piotroski 6/9).
🟢 **Deeply cheap** — P/FCF ~2.6x (scan) / ~5x fwd FCF (live), 70-80% discount to its own historical multiples.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology drift:** "consolidator / synergies / market share" (2024) → "orchestrator and integrator of human + AI" / "augmented by AI" / "resiliency" (2025). The vocabulary migrated from *offense* to *survival*.
- **Capital-allocation language shifted from M&A-hungry to balance-sheet-defensive:** H1-24 boasted of buying back stock *and* hunting acquisitions; by H1-25 the emphasis is "reducing our debt exposure" and "efficiency... to maintain our margin." ZP (EUR490m) was the last big swing; the tone since is consolidate-and-pay-down.
- **New voices say it differently:** Mackenbrock (the incoming co-CEO and de-facto AI spokesman) is markedly more *defensive and ecosystem-oriented* than Rigaudy's old consolidation swagger. The governance reshuffle (Moulay Hafid as Chairman; new AI-credentialed and World-Bank board members; later Sheikha Hanadi Al-Thani as Lead Independent Director, May-2026) signals a board *rebuilding around the AI-credibility problem* — and a notable tilt toward Gulf-sovereign-linked directors, which can presage a strategic anchor investor.
- **Management openly seeded a SoP argument** (H1-25): conceding the niche Specialized Services assets carry "higher valuation benchmarks in the private and public markets" than the consolidated stock reflects — an unusual, near-invitation to the market (or an activist) to re-rate the parts.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (offense→defense), on a financially STABLE but EPS-eroding base.**

Teleperformance is not a melting ice cube *yet* — it throws off ~EUR0.8-1.0bn of FCF, returns it, and grows its 85% core ~3%. But the trajectory is a four-year EPS decline, a *broken* FCF anchor guided lower again, and a specialized-services segment cracking in real time. Management's credibility is clean on *cost* (synergies, buyback) and weak on *demand* (FCF, growth, LanguageLine — 29% beat rate).

**The single most important thing the director must weigh:** This is a **valuation/terminal-decline argument, not a catalyst.** The entire bull case rests on one binary that no transcript can settle — *whether agentic AI structurally hollows out CX/BPO labor-arbitrage volume over 3-5 years, or merely re-mixes it.* The stock is cheap *because* the market has priced meaningful AI hollowing-out; the EPS line (−2.7% 5-yr CAGR) and the −12% Specialized Services print partially *validate* that fear, while the resilient core and EUR900m FCF partially *refute* it. There is no hard date, no spin, no deal, no activist on file that forces resolution. **Size it as a cheap-quality value position against an unresolved structural-disruption tail, not as a special-situation re-rate — and note the re-rate has already partly fired (~+18% to ~EUR63-66 by June-2026).**

---
CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING

bull_thesis: Teleperformance is the global #1 in CX/BPO trading at ~3-5x P/FCF and ~3.8-4.5x EV/EBITDA — a multiple that prices terminal, near-existential decline into a business that still generated ~EUR1.49B EBITDA and EUR901M net free cash flow in a 'bad' FY-25, pays a RISING dividend (EUR4.20->EUR4.50, ~7% yield at EUR63), and runs an active buyback below intrinsic value. The market extrapolates the visibly-shrinking ~15% Specialized Services tail (LanguageLine interpretation + the lost UK visa/TLS contract) onto the whole company, while the ~85% Core Services BPO mass grew through 2025 (LFL 2.3%->3.5%, EMEA/APAC ~5-6%, improved retention; H1-25/FY-25). Three legs support a re-rate: (1) GOVERNANCE RESET — founder Daniel Julien, co-CEO Mackenbrock and CFO Rigaudy stepped down 15-Mar-2026, replaced by outsider CEO Jorge Amar (ex-McKinsey global Digital Customer Care lead from 16-Mar-2026) — the exact restructure-and-crystallize profile; (2) an explicit FY-25 comprehensive STRATEGIC PORTFOLIO REVIEW 'expected to result in a shift in the group scope, including potential asset sales,' with the board acknowledging shareholder demand for increased returns — coded sum-of-parts/breakup language, and management has declined to disclose Specialized Services parts (ZP, AI-data/Agents Only, LLS) that carry 'very high private/public valuation benchmarks' (9-12x to a strategic vs ~4x embedded in TP's stub); (3) a EUR100M+ AI-efficiency program funded by EUR70-90M restructuring protecting margins as the model verticalizes toward stickier back-office/BPO and data-annotation work (both growing double-digit, FY-25). Peer comps reinforce the dislocation: Concentrix ~8-12x FCF, TaskUs higher; TP at ~3-5x is a >50% discount to the oligopoly for the SCALE LEADER. Conditions: Core must re-stabilize (it slipped to -1.7% LFL in Q1-26 — bull needs the promised H2-26 reacceleration); the review must convert to an actual divestiture or a step-up in capital return within ~12 months; AI must prove a margin tailwind (TP keeps the work, cheaper) not a volume cliff. A single niche Specialized sale at a private multiple exposes the Core stub as absurdly cheap and closes the gap toward the ~EUR85.70 street target / ~EUR81 Buffett FV.
bear_thesis: This is a structurally-impaired, AI-disrupted commodity labor-arbitrage business dressed as a deep-value special situation, and the most recent data says the disruption is now reaching the CORE. The cheapness is a value trap on a DECLINING base: net income fell every year (EUR643M->EUR497M), EPS fell 10.77->8.40 (5-yr CAGR -2.7%), FCF fell -26% YoY, and the once-sacred 'EUR1B sustainable FCF' anchor is ALREADY broken in the FY-26 guide of EUR800-850M. Decisive new evidence: in Q1-2026 (reported 28-Apr-2026) CORE SERVICES itself went NEGATIVE at -1.7% LFL and group revenue fell -2.2% LFL — the 'accelerating, resilient' 85% the bull leans on has rolled over, hit by increased offshoring, automation in Trust & Safety, and contract-ramp delays. Trust & Safety is shrinking on explicit client-side automation — the first hard proof AI REMOVES TP revenue rather than adding it, falsifying the 2024 'Gen-AI is our friend' case studies that vanished from the narrative. Specialized Services is in accelerating structural decline (-7%->-12% LFL through 2025) with no date for LanguageLine recovery and zero segment disclosure to value the parts. Leverage went the WRONG way — net debt ~EUR4.43B (post the $490M ZP deal, while buybacks ran) now EXCEEDS the ~EUR3.6B equity cap; Altman-Z 2.8 (grey), Piotroski 6/9, beat rate 2/7 (29%). The catalyst is SOFT and UNDATED: the portfolio review is open-ended; a brand-new CEO (~12 weeks in) has every incentive to kitchen-sink and study rather than act, with no committed divestiture or timeline, and at least one major broker cut its target on the management upheaval and is WAITING for a strategic update before crediting any review upside. The entire institutional memory exited on one day at the most fragile moment, with insiders buying NOTHING (insider ratio 0.00, smart-money ~18/100). What confirms the bear: H2-26 fails to reaccelerate Core; the review concludes with a vague 'we'll optimize' rather than a sale; LLS keeps bleeding on US immigration policy; the FCF base steps down again — at which point '~3-5x P/FCF' re-rates not up but to a permanently-low melting-ice-cube multiple and the stock revisits its ~EUR45.50 52-week low.
consensus_delta: Deep-value branch — is the street pricing terminal decline into a company with stable/growing earnings power? PARTIALLY, and the gap has narrowed. The mispricing thesis is that the market extrapolates the ~15% Specialized Services collapse (LLS/visa) onto the whole company while the ~85% Core BPO mass remained cash-generative and grew ~3% through 2025 — so a ~3-5x FCF multiple on the SCALE LEADER (>50% below Concentrix) prices melting-ice-cube odds the aggregate FCF (EUR901M FY25) does not yet justify. The street assumption most clearly WRONG a year ago — that AI is a pure volume cliff for all of TP — is being partially RIGHTED by the tape: Q1-2026 Core Services went -1.7% LFL on automation/offshoring, so the consensus is no longer simply 'looking the wrong way.' What the street still under-credits: (a) the embedded private-market value of the niche Specialized/AI-data assets management itself flags as worth more than the stub, and (b) the option value of a credible outsider CEO running an explicit portfolio review with board backing for higher returns. The expectations arbitrage is now narrower and two-sided, not the one-way pain trade it was at EUR45.
forcing_function: The re-rating hinges on the new CEO's STRATEGIC PORTFOLIO REVIEW converting to a hard event — but the only dated waypoints are earnings prints, not a binding deal date. Watch: (1) H1-2026 results (~late-Jul / early-Aug 2026) — the first read on whether Core LFL stabilizes and whether Amar attaches a TIMELINE or named asset to the review; this is the nearest forcing function and the specified capitulation/confirmation event. (2) A Capital Markets Day / strategic update under Amar (likely H2-2026) where the portfolio review must produce a concrete divestiture, a quantified SoP, or a capital-return step-up. (3) Any announced sale of a Specialized Services asset (ZP, AI-data/Agents Only, or an LLS carve-out) at a private multiple — the single cleanest catalyst, which would expose the Core stub as mispriced. (4) Activist entry (the setup — cheap scale leader, undisclosed high-value parts, fresh board, founder gone — is textbook Loeb/Bloom) would compress the timeline. None of these is contractually dated, which is exactly why the status is SOFT_EXTENDED.
valley_of_death: Next 3-9 months are fundamentally UGLY and front-loaded with negatives before any catalyst lands. (1) H1-2026 is guided soft with Q1 already below the full-year range (-2.2% LFL); management is betting on an H2-26 reacceleration that, on a 2/7 beat record, is far from assured. (2) EUR70-90M of one-off restructuring charges hit 2026 to fund the EUR100M+ AI program, compressing reported numbers near-term. (3) FY26 net FCF guided DOWN to EUR800-850M — a fresh step-down that, if missed, re-cements the terminal-decline multiple. (4) The levered stub: net debt ~EUR4.43B > equity cap, so even EUR100-150M of incremental FCF erosion or an FX swing (EUR20M sales per EUR0.01 EUR/USD) compounds straight into the equity. (5) LLS remains hostage to US immigration policy with no recovery date. (6) A new CEO's first full strategic update could disappoint if it is 'optimize/study' rather than 'sell' — a classic post-reset air-pocket. The most likely path is a further drawdown toward EUR50-55 (or the EUR45.50 low) on a weak H1 print BEFORE the review delivers anything.
positioning_washout: The mechanical-selling dynamics have largely ALREADY PLAYED OUT, which is both why it is cheap and why the easy washout is behind us. The yield/quality-compounder base that owned TEP at EUR200+ has mostly capitulated (smart-money ~18/100, institutional flow 50/100, stock at 20th percentile of its 52-wk range, below SMA200, near multi-year lows); the management exodus on a single day (15-Mar-2026) was itself the maximum-uncertainty flush, and at least one major broker already cut its target ~50% on it. The remaining washout risk is residual: index/quant trend-followers and any final dividend-focused holders could liquidate on (a) a dividend trim if the review reallocates capital, or (b) ejection from momentum/quality screens after another negative-LFL print. But the dividend is currently RAISED (EUR4.50, ~48% payout, covered), so a forced yield-base exit is not imminent. Net: this is past peak forced-selling — the contrarian setup is real but the 'wait for capitulation' clock is for a fundamental flush (weak H1-26), not a structural deleveraging of the holder base.
moderator_conclusion: TEP is a genuine deep-value dislocation wrapped around a real but SOFT, undated special-situation catalyst — and the regime says to be patient, not aggressive. The setup checks the structural-special-sit boxes the current regime favors (thin-coverage scale leader, governance reset, founder exit, an explicit breakup/portfolio-review with board backing for higher returns, undisclosed parts management concedes are worth more than the stub — a Loeb/Bloom activism template). The VALUE case is strong and largely catalyst-independent: at ~3-5x forward FCF the global #1 trades >50% below a peer oligopoly at 8-12x, the SoP brackets EUR68-78 base with a EUR110 bull, the dividend (~7%) pays you to wait, and the balance sheet (~1.9x EBITDA) is not in solvency danger — hence value_conviction 4. But the CATALYST and TIMING argue for restraint: (1) the regime doc rewards HARD-DATED catalysts inside the favorable window and PENALIZES soft/extended ones — this review is open-ended, single-binary (needs a buyer to materialize), and run by a CEO ~12 weeks in with every incentive to defer; (2) part of the re-rate already FIRED (+~18% off the low to ~EUR63-66), spending some asymmetry; (3) the fundamental tape WORSENED into the catalyst — Q1-26 Core went negative (-1.7% LFL), FY26 FCF guided down, EUR70-90M restructuring hits near-term — so the valley of death (weak H1-26) likely arrives BEFORE the forcing function; (4) resolution-driver independence is a concern — the thesis leans heavily on one shared macro factor (the AI-disruption-to-CX narrative) that also drives the whole peer group. Reconciling: base SoP fair value EUR72 vs live ~EUR63 is only ~+14% with ~-30% downside-to-break — poor base reward/risk — but the catalyst case (review converts) is ~+75% to EUR110. That asymmetry is real but CONDITIONAL and undated. The correct call is WATCHLIST FOR CAPITULATION (B), conviction 3: do not chase the post-reset bounce; wait for the specific flush — a weak H1-2026 print (late-Jul/Aug) that drags the stock back toward EUR50-55 / the EUR45.50 low AND/OR a strategic update that attaches a timeline or named asset to the review. Enter on that confirmation, sized as a cheap-quality special-sit against an unresolved AI tail, not as an aggressive imminent-catalyst trade.

Reward/risk on the BASE case is roughly 0.4-0.5x (poor), but on the catalyst case (review converts to a divestiture) it is ~2.0-2.5x. The asymmetry is entirely conditional on the open, undated strategic review producing an actual asset sale or capital-return step-up; absent that, this is a levered cheap-but-eroding stub with limited base-case edge.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED — The load-bearing catalyst is the governance reset + comprehensive strategic portfolio review (announced at FY-25 results 26-Feb-2026; new CEO Jorge Amar in seat 16-Mar-2026, ex-McKinsey, replacing founder Julien + Mackenbrock). Confirmed live (Jun-2026) but UNDATED and non-binding: the review is open-ended 'expected to result in a shift in group scope, including potential asset sales' with NO committed divestiture, NO timeline, and a CEO only ~12 weeks in with every incentive to study/kitchen-sink before acting; at least one major broker cut its target on the upheaval and is explicitly waiting for a strategic update. interrogator_dossier:
# Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8. Transcripts span 2024-07-30 (H1-24) through 2025-08-01 (H1-25), four consecutive calls, cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory and live FY2025 / FY2026-guidance facts verified on the web (June 2026).*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management tells about Teleperformance has, over these four calls, quietly inverted — from an **offensive consolidation-and-synergy story** to a **defensive "we will survive AI and still print ~EUR1bn of cash" story.**

- **H1-2024 (Jul-24):** The headline is the *Majorel integration* and *scale leadership*. Olivier Rigaudy leads with "acceleration in the growth in Q2," a "smooth integration of Majorel" with "no client lost," and EUR100-150m of run-rate synergies "by '24 and '25." AI is framed explicitly as a *tailwind* — "Gen-AI is helping to get market share," "more than 300 AI projects," with two proof-of-concept case studies showing volume and stickiness *gains*. The posture is: we are the consolidator, AI is our friend, scale wins.
- **FY-2024 (Feb-25):** New co-CEO Thomas Mackenbrock arrives and *broadens* the narrative into a full "AI + EI" (emotional intelligence) strategic frame — "the value of the human connection... is seen more," 500,000 people positioned as the moat. First crack appears: the LanguageLine/visa-services non-renewal is flagged. Capital return is dialed up (EUR500m buyback authorization, dividend raised to EUR4.20, payout to 48%). Still offensive in tone but the AI question is now something they must *answer* rather than *boast about* ("a constant topic of questions from our investors").
- **Q1-2025 (May-25):** The "two tales" structure crystallizes — **strong core services (+2.3% LFL)** vs **deteriorating Specialized Services** (visa loss + LanguageLine US softness). The AI narrative pivots again, now toward a **partnership/ecosystem model** (Sanas, Ema, Parloa) and "we are the orchestrator and integrator of human + AI." The subtext: TP cannot build the frontier AI itself, so it is buying minority stakes and reselling.
- **H1-2025 (Aug-25):** The defensive pivot is complete. Core services still grow (~3% LFL, EMEA/APAC ~5-6%), but Specialized Services *shrinks -12% in Q2*, guidance is cut to "lower end" of the range, and management is reduced to repeating that LanguageLine is "a superstar... that has to weather this period through." Olivier's closing line across these calls hardens into a mantra: **"this company is safe, solid and will continue to deliver a solid free cash flow... roughly EUR1 billion a year."** That is a value-defense, not a growth pitch.

**Drivers introduced and quietly dropped:** Majorel synergies (front and center in 2024, "EUR94m delivered," now largely behind them); offshoring-to-India as a *growth* driver (2024) became, by H1-25, an admission that North America core *declines* "true not just [for us], but for the entire industry." The "300 AI projects → market share" boast of H1-24 is never repeated with quantified revenue impact; by 2025 AI is discussed as something to *defend against* via partnerships.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Acceleration in growth," "on track" | H1-24 | FY24 rev EUR10.28B (+23% reported, but only +2.6% LFL); FY25 rev EUR10.21B, **-0.7% YoY**, 3-yr CAGR +7.8% mostly from the Majorel acquisition | **Poorer than narrative.** Reported growth is M&A; organic is low-single-digit and *decelerating* to 0-2% guided for 2026. |
| "Margin expansion last year" | Feb-25 | Gross margin **11.8% and contracting**; net margin **4.9%**; recurring EBITA margin ~14.8% FY25 | **Mixed.** EBITA margin held ~14.8-15%, but structural gross margin is thin and shrinking; "expansion" is bps-level and FX-flattered. |
| ">EUR1bn FCF... sustainable ~EUR1bn/yr" | Feb-25 / repeated | FY24 FCF EUR1.08bn; **FY25 net FCF EUR901m**; **2026 guided EUR800-850m** | **Narrative is now optimistic.** The "EUR1bn" mantra is already broken — FY25 came in at EUR901m and 2026 is guided *down* to EUR800-850m. FCF -26.1% YoY in the scan. |
| Majorel "smooth," EUR150m synergies | H1-24 → H1-25 | Delivered EUR94m FY24, EUR20-30m more in 2025; debt to ~1.9x EBITDA; net debt EUR4.43B | **Matches.** Synergy delivery is the one claim the numbers corroborate cleanly. But it is now *spent* — a one-time lever, not a forward driver. |
| "AI helps us get market share" | H1-24 | EPS trajectory: 9.36→10.77→10.01→8.71→**8.40**; EPS 5-yr CAGR **-2.7%**; latest-Q EPS YoY **-25%** | **Contradicted by EPS.** Whatever AI is doing to *volume*, earnings power per share has declined four straight years. The market is pricing AI as a threat for a reason the EPS line supports. |
| LanguageLine "healthy, ready to grow again" | H1-25 | Specialized Services -12% LFL in Q2-25; US/immigration-volume sensitive | **Unverified hope.** Management itself admits "recovery... is not happening." This is the segment cracking in real time. |
| Capital return discipline | Feb-25 | EUR500m buyback auth (EUR184m executed FY24); dividend EUR4.20→EUR4.50; payout ~48% | **Matches; genuine green flag** — real cash returned, buyback below intrinsic, dividend covered. |

The cleanest divergence: management's **EUR1bn-FCF anchor is already obsolete** (FY25 EUR901m, FY26 guided EUR800-850m), and the **EPS line has fallen every year** while the narrative insisted on resilience and "acceleration." The story is *richer than the numbers* on growth and AI-as-tailwind; it is *honest and corroborated* on synergies and capital return.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive).**

- **H1-24 — Offensive/confident:** "we are the leader... makes a difference," "Gen-AI is not absolutely an enemy for TP, absolutely not... absolutely the opposite." Rigaudy is selling consolidation upside.
- **Feb-25 — Confident but now *answering*:** Mackenbrock's "these current times of change are good times for TP" is aspirational framing of a *threat*. The very need to dedicate the call to "first glimpses in our new strategy with regard to AI because that has been a constant topic of questions from our investors" signals the market has put them on the back foot.
- **May-25 — Hedged:** "a little bit of a haven of stability... yes, we see also volatility"; the FX sensitivity admission (EUR20m sales per EUR0.01) and the candid "I'm not happy... with the development of LanguageLine."
- **Aug-25 — Defensive:** guidance cut to "lower end"; "we remain cautious on the recovery of this business this year"; "we have to weather through... if you watch the news in the U.S. every day." The shift from *growth/investment* language to *efficiency/cost/macro-headwind* language is unmistakable and monotonic.

Guidance specificity *narrowed defensively*: in 2024 they floated synergy upside ("might come back later... we could probably deliver much more"); by H1-25 Mackenbrock explicitly refuses to narrow guidance — "I cannot narrow down the guidance at this point in time as we are entering a very dynamic situation."

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM.**

- **Synergies — delivered.** EUR100-150m promised H1-24; EUR94m booked FY24, on track to EUR150m. Credible.
- **FCF — over-promised.** The "sustainable ~EUR1bn/yr" anchor (Feb-25) was *missed* the very next year (FY25 EUR901m) and is guided *down again* for 2026 (EUR800-850m). The screener's beat-rate corroborates the trust gap: **beat only 2 of 7 quarters (29%)** — a genuine miss-prone record, not sandbagging.
- **Revenue — serially walked down.** Q1-25 "confirm our financial objectives" → H1-25 "lower end of guidance." They guided optimistically then cut within two quarters.
- **No sandbagging pattern.** The opposite — management consistently set a hopeful anchor (FCF, growth re-acceleration H2, LanguageLine recovery) then under-delivered against it. The 29% beat rate is the tell.

Net: they keep the *promises grounded in already-signed cost actions* (synergies, buyback, dividend) and *miss the promises that depend on demand* (FCF growth, organic re-acceleration, LanguageLine).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **AI cannibalization of volume/pricing** — pushed every call (UBS/Manion on contract pricing H1-24; BofA/Sarli on % of contracts with Gen-AI bots; Deutsche/Wild H1-25 on AI-disruption pace by geography). Answers are consistently *qualitative and deflective* — "too early to give you a precise figure," "still at a small amount, probably in the range of 15%," "no difference in the dynamic." Analysts want a quantified revenue-at-risk number; management never provides one.
- **LanguageLine / Specialized Services** — the topic analysts pushed that *became the bad news*. UBS/Manion (H1-25) had to ask three times to pin down the timing and value of the lost visa contract; management called it "a complex situation." The non-answer in Q1 became a -12% print by Q2.
- **Segment disclosure** — analysts (Deutsche/Wild H1-25) explicitly asked for *constituent-part disclosure of Specialized Services* "to help us value them independently." Management: "there are discussions, but nothing has been decided and disclosed." **This is a live sum-of-parts tell** — management itself concedes the niche specialized assets "echo a higher valuation than we are currently seeing."
- **Americas core decline** — analysts forced the admission that North-American core revenue is in structural top-line decline (offshoring + AI), masked by EMEA/APAC strength.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **EPS down four consecutive years** (10.77→8.40) while the narrative claimed resilience/acceleration.
🔴 **FCF anchor already broken** — "sustainable EUR1bn" → EUR901m FY25 → EUR800-850m guided FY26 (declining).
🔴 **Specialized Services -12% LFL Q2-25**, recovery management itself says "is not happening."
🔴 **29% earnings beat rate** (2/7) — a credibility-eroding miss record.
🔴 **Gross margin 11.8% and contracting** — a thin, labor-arbitrage model directly in AI's blast radius.
🔴 **Net debt EUR4.43B** against a ~EUR3.6B market cap — leverage now *exceeds* equity value; EV is debt-heavy.
🔴 **AI narrative pivoted from "tailwind" to "we'll buy minority stakes and partner"** — a tell they can't build the disruptive tech themselves.

🟢 **Real, covered capital return** — EUR500m buyback authorization, EUR184m executed, dividend raised to EUR4.50, payout ~48%; buyback below intrinsic value.
🟢 **Majorel synergies delivered on schedule** (EUR94m FY24 → EUR150m run-rate) — execution credibility on cost.
🟢 **Core/BPO services (≈85% of revenue) still growing** ~3% LFL with EMEA/APAC +5-7%; the business is not in aggregate decline.
🟢 **Net debt/EBITDA down to ~1.9x** and ~EUR900m-1bn FCF — solvency not in question (Altman-Z 2.8, Piotroski 6/9).
🟢 **Deeply cheap** — P/FCF ~2.6x (scan) / ~5x fwd FCF (live), 70-80% discount to its own historical multiples.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology drift:** "consolidator / synergies / market share" (2024) → "orchestrator and integrator of human + AI" / "augmented by AI" / "resiliency" (2025). The vocabulary migrated from *offense* to *survival*.
- **Capital-allocation language shifted from M&A-hungry to balance-sheet-defensive:** H1-24 boasted of buying back stock *and* hunting acquisitions; by H1-25 the emphasis is "reducing our debt exposure" and "efficiency... to maintain our margin." ZP (EUR490m) was the last big swing; the tone since is consolidate-and-pay-down.
- **New voices say it differently:** Mackenbrock (the incoming co-CEO and de-facto AI spokesman) is markedly more *defensive and ecosystem-oriented* than Rigaudy's old consolidation swagger. The governance reshuffle (Moulay Hafid as Chairman; new AI-credentialed and World-Bank board members; later Sheikha Hanadi Al-Thani as Lead Independent Director, May-2026) signals a board *rebuilding around the AI-credibility problem* — and a notable tilt toward Gulf-sovereign-linked directors, which can presage a strategic anchor investor.
- **Management openly seeded a SoP argument** (H1-25): conceding the niche Specialized Services assets carry "higher valuation benchmarks in the private and public markets" than the consolidated stock reflects — an unusual, near-invitation to the market (or an activist) to re-rate the parts.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (offense→defense), on a financially STABLE but EPS-eroding base.**

Teleperformance is not a melting ice cube *yet* — it throws off ~EUR0.8-1.0bn of FCF, returns it, and grows its 85% core ~3%. But the trajectory is a four-year EPS decline, a *broken* FCF anchor guided lower again, and a specialized-services segment cracking in real time. Management's credibility is clean on *cost* (synergies, buyback) and weak on *demand* (FCF, growth, LanguageLine — 29% beat rate).

**The single most important thing the director must weigh:** This is a **valuation/terminal-decline argument, not a catalyst.** The entire bull case rests on one binary that no transcript can settle — *whether agentic AI structurally hollows out CX/BPO labor-arbitrage volume over 3-5 years, or merely re-mixes it.* The stock is cheap *because* the market has priced meaningful AI hollowing-out; the EPS line (−2.7% 5-yr CAGR) and the −12% Specialized Services print partially *validate* that fear, while the resilient core and EUR900m FCF partially *refute* it. There is no hard date, no spin, no deal, no activist on file that forces resolution. **Size it as a cheap-quality value position against an unresolved structural-disruption tail, not as a special-situation re-rate — and note the re-rate has already partly fired (~+18% to ~EUR63-66 by June-2026).**

---
CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING

bull_thesis:
The bull case starts from valuation. Teleperformance is the world's number-one business-process outsourcer and customer-experience operator (the call-center and back-office work companies hire out), with roughly EUR10.2bn in revenue and about 490k staff. It trades at about 4x forward earnings, about 4x EV/EBITDA (enterprise value, the equity plus net debt, divided by earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization), and about 2.6x price-to-free-cash-flow — a trough against its own roughly 14x ten-year median EV/EBITDA. The cash is real: it still generated EUR1.08bn of free cash flow in 2024 and guides to EUR800-850m for 2026 against a market value of about EUR3.2bn, which is a free-cash yield to shareholders above 20%. The balance sheet is investment-grade (net debt of about 1.9x EBITDA, an Altman-Z score of 2.8, a bankruptcy-risk gauge) and funds an 8% dividend.

Underneath the headline, the engine is steadier than the price implies. The core outsourcing leg — about 86% of revenue — still grew 3-5% on a like-for-like basis (stripping out acquisitions and currency swings) in Europe, the Middle East, Africa and Asia-Pacific through the first half of 2025. The drag is concentrated: a US business being undercut by cheaper offshore work, and a one-off lost UK visa (TLS) contract in Specialized Services — both of which annualize out of the comparisons. Integration of the acquired Majorel business is on plan (EUR94m booked plus another EUR20-30m of a EUR150m target), and a new roughly EUR100m artificial-intelligence cost-savings program is underway.

The setup also offers a path to a re-rating. The February 2026 governance reset swept out founder Julien, deputy Mackenbrock and chief financial officer Rigaudy, and brought in McKinsey AI-care veteran Jorge Amar. Management itself concedes the high-margin Specialized niches carry "high private and public valuations," which hints at a hidden sum-of-the-parts value (valuing each business line separately) and opens the door to an activist or break-up. If AI turns out to complement the business rather than replace it, a re-rate from 4x to even 7-8x earnings — still a deep discount to history — would double the stock. What has to hold for the bull: like-for-like growth stabilizes at zero or better, the EUR800m free-cash-flow floor and the dividend hold, and the AI pivot defends billing rates.
bear_thesis:
The bear's point is blunt: the 4x multiple is correct because the business is structurally melting, not just cyclically depressed. Like-for-like growth (organic, ex-acquisitions and currency) has fallen from +2.6% in 2024 to +1.5% in the first half of 2025 to -2.2% in the first quarter of 2026. The 2026 guide is 0-2% like-for-like, a 14.6% EBITA margin (earnings before interest, tax and amortization) — down from about 15.4% — and EUR800-850m of free cash flow, below the "roughly EUR1bn sustainable" figure that chief financial officer Rigaudy personally staked his credibility on in February 2025.

The earnings record is weak. Earnings per share have fallen every year since 2022 (from 10.77 to 8.40), the company beats its own guidance only 29% of the time (two of seven quarters), and return on invested capital of about 7.9% is at or below the cost of capital — meaning the business earns no economic profit, and the Majorel and ZP acquisitions plus buybacks have not created value.

The real problem is secular, and it is the whole thesis: generative and agentic AI breaks the link between revenue and headcount in exactly Teleperformance's Tier-1 voice support and visa/eligibility-processing businesses. Analysts model a 20-30% net cut in call-center headcount by 2030, and the board effectively ratified the threat by ousting the founder in favor of a McKinsey AI consultant. This is textbook labor-arbitrage deflation: the margin is defended through restructuring (EUR70-90m of 2026 charges) while the top line shrinks. The so-called catalyst is a soft, non-binding event with no contractual payout — multi-year, hanging on a single yes/no AI-turnaround outcome, with no dated trigger — and the CEO-change re-rate already fired in February-March 2026, with the stock near multi-year lows after it. The 8% yield is a distress signal, not a gift; a dividend cut to fund the pivot would mechanically force the yield-focused holders to sell. And net debt of EUR4.4-4.8bn caps whatever a sum-of-the-parts break-up could return to shareholders.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name the test is simple: is the market pricing terminal decline into a company whose earnings power is actually stable? Here the market is largely right, not offside. Consensus — about 4x forward earnings, about 4x EV/EBITDA, an 8% yield — prices structural impairment, and the fundamentals back that up: first-quarter 2026 like-for-like growth of -2.2%, a 2026 guide of 0-2% like-for-like, a 14.6% EBITA margin (down), and EUR800-850m of free cash flow, below the "roughly EUR1bn sustainable" anchor management itself set. The only mild mispricing is that the balance sheet is investment-grade and free cash flow is still substantial, so the equity is not a zero — but there is no hidden stable base for the market to discover. The screener's DCF "fair value 273.5" and Buffett "81.3" figures are stale extrapolations of a base that has since turned negative and should be disregarded. The market is looking the right way.
forcing_function: There is no clean, dated, binding catalyst — nothing on the calendar that forces the re-rating. The governance reset, which would have been that event, already fired in February-March 2026, and the re-rate is spent (the stock is lower since). What remains is soft and multi-year: the new CEO's first strategic plan or any capital-markets day under Amar (not dated), the quarterly like-for-like prints, and the yes/no question of whether AI substitutes for the business or complements it, which resolves over years rather than on a date. The latent sum-of-the-parts or activist break-up of Specialized Services is a possibility management has resisted disclosing, but no activist is on the share register. Without a dated, binding event, this falls short of the bar for a hard, contractual catalyst with a fixed payout.
valley_of_death: The risky window — the next three to nine months — holds several specific hazards. First, the first-half and third-quarter 2026 prints are likely to show continued negative-to-flat like-for-like growth and margin pressure, because EUR70-90m of restructuring charges hit reported free cash flow before the roughly EUR100m run-rate savings land (only about half is booked in 2026). Second, the dividend is at risk: an 8% yield on a falling free-cash-flow base is a live cut candidate, especially if a new McKinsey CEO "kitchen-sinks" results to fund the AI pivot — a cut would be the wash-out event. Third, net debt of EUR4.4-4.8bn means rising rates or refinancing lift interest costs into a shrinking EBITDA. Fourth, the broader macro picture and depressed US LanguageLine volumes persist ("recovery not happening"). There is no cash-burn-to-zero risk here, but free cash flow and the dividend can both be badly cut before any proof of an AI turnaround arrives.
positioning_washout: The shareholder base is heavily tilted toward yield and value buyers — an 8% trailing yield, a 25-year dividend history, and no cut in 16 years. The pivot is the risk to them. If new CEO Amar cuts the dividend to self-fund the AI transformation and pay down debt, the income holders are forced to sell, and that selling would likely overshoot to the downside and create the real entry point. That capitulation has not happened yet — the EUR4.50 dividend is maintained for 2026. The CEO-change shock already cleared out the momentum and growth holders (the stock is near multi-year lows since February 2026). The remaining flush to wait for is a dividend-cut-driven exit by the income holders.
moderator_conclusion:
The verdict is pass for now, conviction 2. Teleperformance is the textbook trap this framework is built to reject: an optical 4x earnings and 2.6x price-to-free-cash-flow on a base that is structurally deflating, not cyclically depressed.

First, identity. The screener's gas-utility peer file is a wrong-company misidentification — corrected here to outsourcing comps (Concentrix and TTEC), against which Teleperformance at about 4x is in-line, not cheap; the apparent discount is only to a stale roughly 14x historical median.

Second, the catalyst has already fired. The founder and CFO ouster and the McKinsey-CEO installation already happened (February-March 2026) and the stock is lower since. The residual AI-turnaround catalyst is a soft, non-binding event with no contractual payout — multi-year, hanging on a single yes/no outcome, and with no dated trigger.

Third, the regime. The Catalyst Watch regime guidance explicitly penalizes theses that hinge on one shared macro factor — AI is the dominant factor in the market right now, and this is its mirror image (AI-displacement) — while it rewards structural special situations in distressed or deleveraging lanes, which this is not. The gap to peers is sector beta (shared sector movement), not single-name alpha.

Fourth, the moat and the secular threat. This is an eroding moat (return on invested capital of about 7.9%, roughly equal to the cost of capital, with falling earnings per share and margins rising while revenue falls) under a material labor-arbitrage-deflation threat that the board itself ratified — which caps the value conviction at 3, and the negative like-for-like growth and dividend-cut risk pull it down to 2. Risk and reward are roughly one-to-one, not the asymmetry required. If anything, this is a future watchlist candidate only after a dividend-cut wash-out flushes the income base near about EUR42 — until then, pass.
sop_breakdown: The base case leans heavily on the bear's sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately), given negative like-for-like growth, margin-down guidance, a catalyst that has already fired, and an eroding moat. Core Services — about EUR8.8bn of revenue at roughly a 12.5% EBITA margin (about EUR1.10bn) — at a blended roughly 5.5x EBITA (a midpoint between the de-rated peer group at 4x and a stabilization at 7x) is worth about EUR6.0bn. Specialized Services — about EUR1.4bn of revenue at roughly 9x EBITA (about EUR0.35bn) — is worth about EUR3.2bn, after a haircut for LanguageLine and visa volatility. That gives a gross enterprise value of about EUR9.2bn; less net debt of EUR4.4bn leaves about EUR4.8bn of equity across roughly 58m shares, or about EUR83 on the optimistic blend. But de-rate that to about EUR61 once you apply the realistic peer multiple to the shrinking base and discount the still-unproven AI defense. The premium to the EUR55-56 spot price reflects the residual, still-real free cash flow plus the investment-grade balance sheet — not a clean discount.
risk_reward: Risk and reward are roughly symmetric, about one-to-one. The downside to a break is about EUR42 (a harsh labor-arbitrage sum-of-the-parts, roughly -24% from EUR55.56), against an upside to fair value of about EUR61-64 (roughly +10-15%). The bull's about EUR129 exists only if AI is fully de-risked. This is not the two-to-three-to-one asymmetry a special situation demands; the live de-rated peer multiple shows the market already pays peer-group value, leaving no clean margin of safety (the cushion between price and conservative worth).
peer_comps_note: The screener's peer-group file misidentifies TEP.PA as "Terega" / French regulated gas infrastructure (Enagas, Snam, Fluxys, at 12-20x EV/EBITDA) — that is the wrong company, and its anchor is discarded. TEP.PA is Teleperformance, an outsourcing and customer-experience operator; the correct comps are Concentrix (CNXC) and TTEC, which trade at about 4-5x earnings and about 4x EV/EBITDA today (June 2026). Teleperformance at about 4x is in-line with this de-rated group, not cheap to it — the whole outsourcing cohort repriced together on shared AI-displacement fear. By the convergence rule this gap is sector beta (a shared, unresolved AI-substitution driver), not single-name alpha; the roughly 14x historical median is a stale anchor that inflates the apparent discount. No name-specific edge is credited from the peer gap.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — Teleperformance SE (TEP.PA)

**Identity correction (this matters):** TEP.PA is **Teleperformance SE** — the world's largest outsourcing and customer-experience (call-center) operator (Majorel, LanguageLine, ZP, about 490k employees). It is **not** "Terega" / French regulated gas infrastructure. The screener's peer-group file (Enagas, Snam, Fluxys) is a wrong-company misidentification, and its "regulated-utility, 12-20x EV/EBITDA" anchor is discarded throughout this dossier. The correct comp set is outsourcing and customer-experience: **Concentrix (CNXC)**, TTEC, and the offshore IT-services cohort.

---

## 1. How the story has changed

Across eight quarters of transcripts (first half of 2024 through first half of 2025) the story bends in one direction: **from a growth-and-synergy story to a defensive AI-survival story.**

- **First half of 2024 (Rigaudy):** Offensive tone. "Acceleration in the growth in Q2," Majorel integration "smooth," EUR100-150m of synergies confirmed, 300 AI projects "helping to get market share." AI is framed explicitly as a tailwind: "Gen-AI is helping to get market share... it's absolutely the opposite [of an enemy]." Reported growth was +28.2% (inflated by Majorel), with like-for-like growth only +1.7%.
- **Full-year 2024 (February 2025, Mackenbrock joins):** Peak confidence on cash. "EUR10bn revenue for the first time," "more than EUR1.08bn free cash flow," dividend raised to EUR4.20, a EUR500m buyback, ZP acquired ($490m). But the AI question is now the constant topic of investor questions — management is on the back foot, building a "human + AI is complementary" defense. The visa-contract non-renewal (LanguageLine/TLS) first surfaces as "a little bit dip."
- **First quarter 2025 through first half of 2025:** The "two tales" framing hardens. Core services growing about 3-5% like-for-like, **Specialized Services collapsing** (-12% like-for-like in the second quarter of 2025) on the lost visa contract plus soft US LanguageLine volumes ("we anticipated a recovery in the second half. Which is not happening"). Capital allocation pivots from buyback-and-dividend to **AI partnerships** (Sanas, Ema, Parloa) — minority stakes — and the "Future Forward" / TP.ai FAB platform is unveiled at the June 2025 capital-markets day.

**Quietly dropped:** the early "synergy upside to EUR150m" optimism, and the framing of offshoring as a pure positive (offshoring is now openly described as *cannibalizing* the North America top line).

**Newly emphasized:** AI as existential. The arc ends with management insisting the core is "resilient" and "a haven of stability" — defensive vocabulary that did not exist in the first half of 2024.

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |

| "Sustainable free cash flow of roughly EUR1bn a year... this company is safe, solid" | February 2025 | Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow 2.77B (FMP, mixed) but full-year free cash flow **-26% year over year**; full-year 2025 revenue -0.7%; **2026 guide cut to EUR800-850m free cash flow (excluding restructuring)** | **Diverges** — the EUR1bn floor was breached within a year; the 2026 guide is below it |
; Growth "accelerating," mid-single-digit medium-term; Full-year 2024 through first half of 2025; Like-for-like deceleration: +2.6% (2024) -> +1.5% (first half 2025) -> **-2.2% (first quarter 2026)**; 2026 guide **0-2%**; **Diverges hard** — the actual trajectory is into contraction; 
| Margin "expansion," resilient | First half of 2025 | Gross margin 11.8% (contracting); net 4.9%; **2026 EBITA margin guided down to 14.6%** (from about 15.4%) | **Diverges** — margin is being defended with restructuring, not expanding |
| Earnings per share / earnings power intact | 2021-2025 | EPS **9.36 -> 8.40** (5-year compound annual rate -2.7%); net income 557M -> 497M; latest-quarter EPS **-25% year over year** | **Diverges** — earnings power has eroded every year since 2022 |
| Capital return (buyback plus rising dividend) | Full-year 2024 | EUR500m buyback plus dividend EUR4.20 -> EUR4.50; but return on invested capital only **7.9%** (about equal to the cost of capital) and net debt **EUR4.4-4.8B** | **Matches the cash deployment, but the returns don't justify it** — buying back stock at a 4x earnings multiple is rational only if the base is stable, which it is not |

**Net:** the *cash* and *synergy* claims are roughly true; the *growth, margin, and "safe EUR1bn FCF"* claims have been overtaken by events. The screener's DCF "fair value 273.5" is a stale extrapolation of a base that is now shrinking — it must be disregarded.

## 3. Tone and confidence — **declining**

- First half of 2024: offensive, "we are the leader... makes a difference even in a volatile environment." Rigaudy bullish on synergy upside.
- Full-year 2024: still confident on cash ("this company is safe, solid"), but visibly *managing* the AI narrative for investors.
- First quarter / first half of 2025: defensive. "Haven of stability... ocean of volatility"; "weather through"; LanguageLine recovery "not happening." Guidance moved to the **low end** of the range in the first half of 2025.
- The shift from **offence (growth/market-share)** to **defence (efficiency/cost/resilience/restructuring)** is unmistakable and steady.

## 4. Guidance credibility — **low-to-medium**

- The earnings-beat rate is **2 of 7 quarters (29%)** — a genuine miss pattern, not sandbagging.
- The full-year 2024 guide for a second-half-2025 acceleration **did not materialize** (first-half-2025 like-for-like +1.5%, Specialized -12%).
- The "sustainable about EUR1bn FCF" promise (February 2025) was cut to **EUR800-850m** within twelve months (first-quarter-2026 guide).
- A LanguageLine "recovery in the second half" was called repeatedly, then management admitted it "is not happening."
- Reliability: **low.** Management has serially guided to a second-half recovery that does not arrive, and the headline EUR1bn FCF anchor was broken. Credit only the operational, synergy and cost-discipline claims.

## 5. Where analysts pressed

- **AI / headcount displacement** — pushed on every call (UBS, BofA, Deutsche). Management deflects to "human + AI complementary," "300 AI projects," partnerships — never quantifies revenue-per-head erosion or the net headcount trajectory. *This is the deflection that later became the thesis* (the February 2026 CEO ouster).
- **LanguageLine / US volumes** — analysts (Deutsche's Wild, BofA) pressed on the divergence; management blamed "the situation in the US" (immigration/LA) and promised a second-half recovery that failed.
- **Specialized Services disclosure** — Deutsche asked for segment-level disclosure to value the high-multiple niche assets independently; management declined ("nothing decided"). The refusal to break out the parts is itself a tell.
- **Pricing under AI** — analysts probed whether AI compresses billing rates; management insisted "it helps pricing dramatically" — unverifiable and contradicted by flat-to-declining revenue.

## 6. Red flags and green flags

🔴 Like-for-like growth has gone **negative** (first quarter 2026 -2.2%) against a guided 0-2% — the screener's "stable regulated revenue" read is wrong.
🔴 **2026 FCF guide EUR800-850m**, below the "sustainable EUR1bn" management staked its credibility on.
🔴 EBITA margin guided **down** to 14.6%; the model is now margin-up-while-revenue-down (the classic late-cycle labor-arbitrage tell).
🔴 **Founder-CEO Daniel Julien plus deputy Mackenbrock plus CFO Rigaudy all ousted (March 2026)**, replaced by a McKinsey AI-care consultant (Jorge Amar) — a board panic-button on AI disruption, not a planned succession.
🔴 Net debt EUR4.4-4.8B against a falling FCF base; return on invested capital 7.9%, about equal to the cost of capital (no economic profit).
🔴 An 8% trailing dividend yield — the market is pricing distress or a possible cut, not a bargain.
🔴 EPS down every year 2022 through 2025; beat rate 29%.
🟢 EUR1.08bn FCF actually delivered in 2024; the balance sheet is still investment-grade (net debt about 1.9x EBITDA), Altman-Z 2.8 (grey, not distress).
🟢 Majorel synergies on plan; cost discipline real; about EUR100m of AI run-rate savings targeted for 2026.
🟢 Core (outsourcing) services still growing about 3-5% like-for-like in Europe, the Middle East, Africa and Asia-Pacific — the franchise is not dead, only the offshore-cannibalized US and Specialized legs.

## 7. Hidden signals

- Terminology migrated from "growth/market-share" to "resilience/efficiency/weather through/restructuring." The word "transformation" now appears as a *cost line* (EUR70-90m of restructuring in 2026), not an opportunity.
- Capital allocation quietly shifted from **buyback/dividend** to **AI-startup minority stakes** ($13m Sanas, Ema, Parloa) — small absolute sums, but a signal that management itself doesn't believe organic investment defends the moat.
- The new CFO / deputy-CEO duo (Mackenbrock, ex-Majorel) leaned harder into "we are an orchestrator and integrator of human + AI" — a re-framing of the *same* labor-arbitrage business to sound platform-like. The February 2026 installation of a McKinsey AI consultant as CEO is the board conceding the in-house pivot was insufficient.
- The refusal to disclose Specialized-Services parts (despite "high private-market valuations") suggests the parts are worth more than the whole — a latent sum-of-the-parts / activist hook, but no activist is yet present.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Trajectory: deteriorating (pivoting under duress).** The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: **is the optical 4x earnings a fear-print on a still-EUR800m+ FCF franchise, or a value trap on a base that AI structurally deflates?** The evidence — negative like-for-like growth, margin-down guidance, an FCF cut, the founder/CFO ouster, an 8% yield — points to a **structurally-shrinking base**, where the right multiple is genuinely low, not a mispricing.

**Moat:** an intangible-brand and scale-cost edge in outsourcing delivery, historically narrow (switching costs are low; this is a labor-arbitrage spread business). The returns evidence — **return on invested capital 7.9%, about equal to the cost of capital, falling EPS, contracting gross margin, margin-up-while-revenue-down** — makes the moat **eroding**, not wide. **Moat trend: eroding.**

**Secular threat:** **material, trending toward terminal.** Generative and agentic AI voice agents break the link between revenue and headcount in exactly the businesses (Tier-1 voice support, eligibility/visa processing) that fund Teleperformance's spread. Industry analysts project a 20-30% net call-center headcount reduction by 2030. The board's own action (firing the founder for an AI consultant) ratifies the threat. This is **not** a cyclical dip — the cheapness is a value-trap-leaning fear-print: real downside protection from a still-large FCF base and an investment-grade balance sheet, but a melting terminal base that caps how much of the optical discount is real edge.

Management credibility 2 of 5; trajectory deteriorating; eroding moat; moat trend eroding; secular threat material.
